title: Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits
author: Philip A. Fisher
subtitle: The Original Treatise on Growth Investing and the 'Scuttlebutt' Method
description: The classic text on growth investing that influenced Warren Buffett and generations of investors. Fisher introduced the 'scuttlebutt' method — gathering competitive intelligence through interviews with customers, competitors, and suppliers — to evaluate companies. He presented fifteen points for identifying superior growth companies and argued for holding great companies for the long term rather than trading in and out.
keyIdeas: The scuttlebutt method: gather intelligence from customers, competitors, and suppliers
Fifteen points for identifying superior growth companies
Invest only in companies with sustainable competitive advantages
Hold great companies for the long term — decades, not quarters
R&D spending must translate to profitable products
Sales organization quality is as important as product quality
Management depth and integrity are critical investment criteria
Short-term earnings fluctuations are irrelevant for quality companies
Don't over-diversify — concentrated positions in best ideas outperform
The best time to buy is when a quality company has a temporary, fixable problem
keyTakeaways: The 'scuttlebutt' method — talking to customers, competitors, and employees — reveals more than financial statements
Fifteen qualitative criteria identify companies with sustainable growth potential
R&D effectiveness matters more than R&D spending — what has been commercialized?
Sales and marketing capability is a critical competitive advantage often overlooked
Management integrity is non-negotiable — honest management treats shareholders as partners
Hold great companies for the long term; short-term trading is a loser's game
Don't over-diversify — a concentrated portfolio of your best ideas outperforms
Buy quality companies during temporary, fixable problems
Ignore short-term earnings noise — focus on the long-term trajectory
Investing is about companies, not stocks — understand the business, not the ticker
whoShouldRead: Growth investors looking for a qualitative, research-intensive framework
Value investors who want to understand the complementary approach
Anyone who researches stocks through primary sources (interviews, site visits)
Long-term investors who believe in concentrated, high-conviction portfolios

relatedBooks.title: A Random Walk Down Wall Street
